metadata.title: Corruption Cases in Panama - Typology and Frequency
metadata.description: Classification of corruption types in Panama by frequency, based on research from Transparency International, CIEPS, Panama's Attorney General, and investigative journalism sources.

corruption_perception_index_2024.note: Lowest score in 12 years, on par with Bosnia-Herzegovina, Nepal, and Sierra Leone

most_common.description: Manipulation of public bidding processes, including direct contracting without competition, collusion with contractors, overpriced contracts, and unjustified contract modifications that inflate costs after award.

notable_cases.name: Odebrecht
notable_cases.summary: Brazilian firm paid $59M in bribes (2010–2014) to secure contracts; cost overruns exceeded $2 billion USD.

notable_cases.name: FCC Construcción
notable_cases.summary: Spanish company Fomento de Construcciones y Contratas paid over 82 million euros in bribes for Panamanian contracts.

notable_cases.name: Servicios Blue Apple
notable_cases.summary: Eight people sentenced in 2023, including ex-minister Federico Suárez (14 years; $27.4M fine).

notable_cases.name: Panama Ports Company
notable_cases.summary: State losses estimated at over $1.2 billion USD due to unfavorable contractual modifications.

most_common.description: Diversion of public funds by officials for personal benefit. Common in the Social Security Fund (CSS), municipal governments, Ministry of Education, and Ministry of Housing.

notable_cases.name: Casos Gobierno Cortizo (2024)
notable_cases.summary: Panama's Anti-Corruption Prosecutors Office opened multiple embezzlement cases from the Cortizo administration (2019–2024).

notable_cases.name: Ricardo Martinelli
notable_cases.summary: Former president accused in 2018 of embezzling millions in public funds used to acquire luxury properties.

most_common.description: Panama is a global money laundering hub due to its dollarized economy, large banking sector, Colón Free Zone (one of the world's largest free-trade zones), and permissive financial regulation historically.

notable_cases.name: Panama Papers (2016)
notable_cases.summary: Leak of 11.5 million files from law firm Mossack Fonseca exposed global use of Panamanian shell companies to conceal wealth. 28 defendants acquitted by Panamanian court in 2024.

notable_cases.name: Martinelli Linares (Odebrecht)
notable_cases.summary: Luis Enrique and Ricardo Martinelli Linares pleaded guilty in the US to money laundering conspiracy, receiving $28M+ in bribe proceeds via offshore accounts.

notable_cases.name: Vuelo de Dinero / PDVSA
notable_cases.summary: A $1.2B corruption scheme linking Venezuelan state oil company PDVSA to Panamanian financial networks, prosecuted in the US, Spain, Argentina, and Panama.

most_common.description: Payments to officials to secure government contracts, obtain permits, or influence regulatory and judicial decisions. Particularly prevalent in construction, customs, and the judiciary.

Partisan appointments in public institutions, using personal and political connections to steer contracts, jobs, or resources. Documented extensively in IFARHU, AIG, municipalities, and the Ministry of Education.
Drug trafficking organizations have historically infiltrated Panama's police and security services, recruiting or bribing officers to enable drug shipments and other criminal activity.

most_common.type_en: Emergency Fund Abuse (COVID-19 and Crisis Situations)
most_common.description: During the COVID-19 pandemic, emergency procurement rules were exploited: direct contracting without competition, purchases at inflated prices, acquisitions misaligned with market value, and opaque distribution of supplies.

most_common.type: Captura del Estado / Corrupción Política Sistémica
most_common.description: Private or political interests systematically controlling government institutions, regulatory bodies, and legislative processes to extract rents and redirect public resources.

least_common.type: Corrupción de Baja Escala / Coimas Burocráticas
least_common.description: Small payments to low-level officials to speed up routine procedures. Panama has notably low levels of petty corruption compared to other countries in the region, attributed to low bureaucratic red tape and relatively high GDP per capita.
least_common.note: Panama is explicitly characterized by high grand corruption and low petty corruption in academic and policy literature.

least_common.description: Direct manipulation of election results or vote counts. While vote-buying and political patronage exist, outright electoral fraud in vote tallying is rarely documented.
Bribery of individual lower court judges or clerks by private citizens. High-level judicial capture is more common; direct bribery of individual judges in ordinary citizen cases is less documented.

least_common.type: Extorsión Directa a Ciudadanos por Funcionarios
least_common.type_en: Direct Extortion of Citizens by Officials
least_common.description: Systematic extortion of ordinary citizens by public servants demanding payments for basic services. Less prevalent than in neighboring countries due to Panama's relatively higher income levels and lower bureaucratic friction.

Corruption between purely private companies (excluding their dealings with the state). While corporate governance issues exist, private-to-private corruption is much less documented than public sector corruption.

summary.dominant_pattern: Grand corruption — large-scale schemes involving the state, major contractors, and political elites — is the defining feature of Panamanian corruption. Public procurement fraud is by far the most impactful and frequent type.
summary.least_common_pattern: Petty corruption (small bureaucratic bribes) is unusually rare for a Latin American country of Panama's income level, mainly because citizens interact less frequently with bureaucratic checkpoints.
key_structural_drivers: Colon Free Zone enabling opaque trade flows
Permissive corporate registry historically used for shell companies
Political financing gaps enabling quid-pro-quo relationships
Revolving door between private sector and public officials
